chapters.summary: Arjuna, seeing his kinsmen arrayed for battle, is overcome by grief and doubt, and surrenders to Krishna seeking guidance.
The heart of the Gita: the eternal nature of the soul, the duty of action without attachment to results, and the marks of a steady, settled mind.
Why action is unavoidable, and how to act selflessly — performing one's duty as an offering, free from attachment, leads to liberation.
Krishna reveals transcendental knowledge and why the divine takes form age after age to restore dharma.
Renunciation of action and selfless action lead to the same goal — the wise act without attachment and remain untouched by sin.
The practice of meditation — disciplining the restless mind, and uniting the self with the Supreme.
Krishna as the source and sustaining force of all that exists, and the four kinds of seekers who turn to the divine.
The importance of the final thought at death, and constant remembrance of the divine as the path beyond rebirth.
The royal knowledge: loving devotion offered to the Supreme frees one from the bonds of the material world.
Krishna describes his infinite manifestations and opulences to deepen Arjuna's devotion.
Granted divine sight, Arjuna beholds the awe-inspiring universal form of God containing all of creation.
The supremacy of loving devotion, and the qualities of the devotee who is dear to God.
The distinction between the perishable body (the field) and the eternal soul (the knower of the field).
Goodness, passion and ignorance — the three modes that bind us, and how devotion carries us beyond them.
The eternal tree of material existence, and Krishna as the Supreme Person beyond the perishable and imperishable.
The contrast between divine and demoniac qualities, and why following scripture and goodness elevates the soul.
Faith, food, sacrifice, austerity and charity in the three modes — and the sacred meaning of Om Tat Sat.
The grand conclusion: true renunciation, acting by one's nature, and the highest secret — surrender in love to the divine.
